title: Rain Shelf
desc: Even in Everrain, the workers keep one narrow ledge dry enough for tea and argument.

steps.text:
There is one ledge in Everrain that locals refer to as the dry shelf, which is only slightly true and therefore close enough. A stone overhang catches most of the rain. Hanging cloths take care of the rest. Beneath them, workers keep a cup rack, a ring of warming stones, and a row of hooks where damp gloves and belts can drip in peace.

By midday the shelf becomes the nearest thing Everrain has to a public square. Bridge crews stop there between jobs. Fungus pickers warm their hands over the stones. Tunnel watchers argue about whether the water from the upper lake tastes different after heavy weather. Nobody says the shelf is important in a dramatic voice. They just keep maintaining it, which tells you everything.

Today the drip cloths have sagged and the cup shelf has grown slick with algae. A shelf hand lifts two folded fabrics and a scrub brush. "Keep the rain out, or keep the tea off the moss. Either helps."

options.label: Hang fresh drip cloths along the ledge
Scrub the cup shelf and warming ring

steps.text:
The new cloths are waxed reed-fiber, heavy enough to hang straight and light enough to sway instead of tearing when the draft changes. You tie them one by one along the overhang, adjusting their angles until the runoff starts falling in neat threads off the outer edge instead of blowing inward onto the benches.

As the shelf dries, the place changes immediately. Steam from the tea pot stops mingling with cold spray. The bench planks darken more slowly. A pair of wet tunnel workers step in from the ledge, look up at the new cloth line, and give the kind of approving grunt reserved for improvements that save discomfort without asking for thanks.

By the time the shelf hand pours the next round of bitter reed tea, the whole space feels tighter, warmer, and more deliberate. Everrain is full of grand wet beauty. It is also full of creatures working hard to carve out one reasonably dry place to sit inside it.

options.label: Take a cup and enjoy the shelter

steps.text:
The cup shelf is carved directly into the wall, and the warming ring is just a shallow bowl of stone filled with heat bricks kept carefully short of scorching. Both have gone slick with green film from the constant damp. You set to work with the brush while the shelf hand turns the cups upside down to dry.

As the algae comes away, details emerge: little scratch marks where generations of mugs have been set down, a groove worn by someone always resting the tea pot in the same place, the blackened edge of the ring where heat has licked stone for years without ever winning. The shelf hand notices you looking. "This ledge keeps habits," she says. "That is how you know it belongs to people."

When the last cup is racked and the warming stones are clean, the first break crowd arrives. They come in dripping, tired, and immediately opinionated about water, weather, and tunnel rumors. The shelf holds them all without fuss. In Everrain, that is a kind of comfort worth maintaining.

options.label: Leave the shelf ready for the next shift
